Ecclesiastes 10 confronts us with a unsettling truth: the fragility of wisdom and the pervasiveness of folly. One dead fly ruins an entire vat of the perfumer's most valuable work. One act of foolishness can obliterate a lifetime of accumulated honor and reputation. The Teacher is not offering comfort here but a warning that our character is not as secure as we might wish. A fool cannot hide his nature—he broadcasts it with every step, every word, every decision. Yet folly is not confined to the obviously incompetent. What troubles the Teacher most is that in this world under the sun, folly is often elevated to power and prominence. Kings lack sense; fools wear crowns; the unworthy sit on thrones while the capable languish in obscurity. This is not how the world should work, and the Teacher's bewilderment at this reversal cuts deep. We live in a reality where the wrong people often hold the wrong positions, where wisdom goes unheeded and foolishness flourishes. This is not an accident or an aberration—it is "an evil seen under the sun," a fundamental disorder in the world we inhabit.

What the Teacher reveals about folly is that it is fundamentally self-destructive. The fool cannot escape the consequences of his own actions. He digs his own pit and falls in; he breaches walls and encounters danger at every turn. He works himself weary pursuing ends he cannot clearly see, wandering toward a destination he doesn't recognize. His words, unchecked and multiplied, eventually consume him—the lips that cannot stay silent ensure his own destruction. Even when a fool does not intend harm, his very nature draws harm toward him. The Teacher is describing not merely poor judgment but a condition of being fundamentally disconnected from how reality actually works. A fool does not know "the way to the city," meaning he lacks the basic wisdom to navigate life itself. Meanwhile, a wise person operates with restraint and discernment. When provoked, the wise choose calmness because they understand that composure can lay great offenses to rest. The wise know that skill and proper technique matter as much as raw effort—a sharp blade wielded with knowledge accomplishes more than strength without wisdom.

The Teacher also turns his attention to power and prosperity, and his observations should unsettle anyone who assumes wealth or position is a sign of God's favor or personal virtue. Money answers everything in this corrupted world—it is the path to influence, pleasure, and escape—yet this very fact reveals how broken our condition is. A land whose king is young and undisciplined, whose leaders feast in the morning in drunkenness rather than self-control, faces ruin. But even a properly ordered kingdom, one led by those of noble character who feast at the proper time for strength and not excess, remains fragile. Laziness causes the roof to cave in; a careless hand allows the house to leak. Life itself requires constant vigilance and appropriate effort. Finally, the Teacher warns us even about our private thoughts. Do not curse the king in your thoughts, he says, because you cannot be certain of privacy. A bird of the air may carry your words. In a world where folly rises to prominence and wisdom is sidelined, discretion becomes essential. This is not a call to spineless compliance but a sober recognition that unguarded speech in an unjust world can bring ruin upon yourself.

These chapters do not offer us the comfort of believing that wisdom always wins or that the righteous always prosper visibly in this age. Instead, they call us to maintain wisdom despite a world that often rewards foolishness, to live with restraint and discernment even when others succeed through excess, and to understand that our only true security lies not in the order of this world but in a reverence for God that the Preacher has yet fully to reveal. We are invited to see the disorder for what it is—not reason for despair, but reason for honest assessment of where our real hope must rest.
